claim_text: Finland's average temperature has risen by 2.3 degrees Celsius since pre-industrial times

evidence.content: Finland has warmed approximately 2.3°C since the late 1800s, roughly twice the global average rate.
High-latitude regions including Scandinavia have experienced warming 2-3 times the global average.

expected_verdict.justification: Multiple authoritative sources confirm Finland has warmed approximately 2.3°C since pre-industrial times, consistent with Arctic amplification patterns documented by IPCC.

expected_insight_summary: This claim is well-supported by both national meteorological data and international climate assessments. The 2.3°C figure is consistent with documented Arctic amplification effects.

claim_text: 2024 was the warmest year on record for Finland

evidence.content: 2024 annual mean temperature data shows record warmth across most Finnish monitoring stations.
2024 was confirmed as the warmest year globally, with particularly extreme warmth in northern Europe.

expected_verdict.justification: Both Finnish national data and European climate services confirm 2024 as a record warm year for Finland.

claim_text: Summer temperatures reached unprecedented highs of 35.2°C in Lapland

evidence.content: The highest recorded temperature in Lapland in 2024 was 33.8°C at Utsjoki in July.
Finland's all-time record high is 37.2°C set in Liperi in 2010. Lapland's record is approximately 34.3°C.

expected_verdict.justification: The specific figure of 35.2°C in Lapland is not supported by official station data. While temperatures were unusually high, the exact figure appears inaccurate.

claim_text: Germany's renewable energy sector generated 52% of the country's electricity in 2024

evidence.content: Renewable energy sources covered approximately 52-55% of German net electricity generation in 2024.
German renewable electricity share reached a new high in 2024, exceeding 50% for the first time on an annual basis.

expected_verdict.justification: Multiple German energy institutions confirm renewable electricity generation exceeded 50% in 2024, making the 52% figure consistent with official data.

claim_text: Wind power contributed 28% of Germany's total electricity generation

evidence.content: Wind energy (onshore + offshore) contributed approximately 27-29% of Germany's net electricity generation in 2024.

expected_verdict.justification: The 28% figure falls within the range reported by Fraunhofer ISE for combined onshore and offshore wind generation.

claim_text: Germany aims to achieve 80% renewable electricity by 2030

evidence.content: The amended Renewable Energy Sources Act (EEG 2023) sets a target of 80% renewable electricity by 2030.
Germany's National Energy and Climate Plan confirms the 80% renewables target for 2030.

expected_verdict.justification: The 80% target is enshrined in German law (EEG 2023) and confirmed by the European Commission.

claim_text: There were several near-blackout incidents in Germany in January 2024

evidence.content: The German Federal Network Agency reported no critical grid stability events in January 2024.
European grid frequency remained within normal bounds throughout January 2024.

expected_verdict.justification: Neither the German grid regulator nor European transmission system data support claims of near-blackout incidents in January 2024.

expected_insight_summary: This claim lacks support from official grid stability monitoring data. While grid management challenges exist, no near-blackout events were officially recorded.

claim_text: Mediterranean sea levels will rise by 20-30 centimeters by 2050

evidence.content: Under SSP2-4.5, global mean sea level rise is projected at 15-30 cm by 2050 relative to 1995-2014 baseline.
Mediterranean regional sea level trends show slightly above global average rates of rise.

expected_verdict.justification: The 20-30 cm range is consistent with IPCC projections under moderate emission scenarios, particularly given the Mediterranean's above-average regional trends.

claim_text: Only 15% of at-risk coastal municipalities have implemented comprehensive protection plans

evidence.content: Coastal adaptation implementation varies widely across Europe, with many municipalities lacking comprehensive plans.

expected_verdict.justification: While low adaptation rates are documented, the specific 15% figure could not be verified against any authoritative database.

claim_text: Norway's sovereign wealth fund divested $7.6 billion from fossil fuel companies in 2024

evidence.content: The Government Pension Fund Global reported significant fossil fuel divestments in 2024.

expected_verdict.justification: While significant divestments occurred, the exact $7.6 billion figure requires verification against the fund's official annual report.

expected_insight_summary: The fund has made notable fossil fuel divestments, but the precise dollar amount cited requires confirmation from official NBIM reporting.

claim_text: Norway is Europe's largest oil and gas producer

evidence.content: Norway is the largest oil producer in Western Europe and a major natural gas exporter.
Norway accounts for the largest share of crude oil production in Europe outside of Russia.

expected_verdict.justification: Norway is confirmed as Western Europe's largest oil producer and a major gas exporter, though the claim depends on whether Russia is included in 'Europe'.

claim_text: Herring catches in the Baltic Sea have declined by 40% over the past decade

evidence.content: ICES advisory reports show significant declines in Baltic herring stocks, with catches reduced by approximately 30-45% depending on sub-basin.
Baltic herring biomass has shown a declining trend over the past 15 years.

expected_verdict.justification: ICES data confirms substantial herring catch declines in the 30-45% range, making the 40% figure consistent with observed trends.

claim_text: The Baltic Sea's surface temperature has increased by 1.5°C since 1990

evidence.content: Baltic Sea surface temperatures have risen by approximately 1-2°C since the early 1990s, with the highest increases in the Bothnian Bay.
Sea surface temperature trends in the Baltic show warming of approximately 0.3-0.6°C per decade since 1990.

expected_verdict.justification: Both HELCOM and Copernicus data support warming of 1-2°C since 1990 in the Baltic, making the 1.5°C figure within the documented range.

claim_text: Oxygen-depleted dead zones cover approximately 70,000 square kilometers of the Baltic seabed

evidence.content: Oxygen-depleted areas in the Baltic Sea have been estimated at 60,000-80,000 km² in recent years.
Baltic dead zones have expanded significantly, covering areas comparable in size to Ireland.

expected_verdict.justification: The 70,000 km² figure falls within HELCOM's documented range of 60,000-80,000 km² for Baltic oxygen-depleted zones.

claim_text: The Finnish government has committed to carbon neutrality by 2035

evidence.content: Finland's Climate Act sets a carbon neutrality target for 2035.
Finland has the most ambitious carbon neutrality target in the EU, aiming for 2035.

expected_verdict.justification: Finland's 2035 carbon neutrality target is enshrined in the Finnish Climate Act and widely recognized as the EU's most ambitious national target.

expected_insight_summary: Finland's 2035 carbon neutrality commitment is well-documented in national legislation and recognized internationally as the EU's most ambitious timeline.
